The labial surface of the lower left central incisor (#31) and the incisal edge of the lower left lateral incisor (#32) are also stained with a brownish‑yellow discolor that matches the pigment seen elsewhere in the mouth. On the cervical third of several teeth, a diffuse yellowish hue can be observed, suggesting early dentin exposure or extrinsic staining at the neck of the crowns. A noticeable accumulation of dental plaque and calculus is present on the occlusal and proximal surfaces, appearing as a rough, whitish‑gray layer that follows the grooves and fiss of the teeth. The gingiva throughout the arches is generally dark red, consistent with mild inflammation, except where recession is evident; the gingival margin recedes apically around the lower left premolar region, exposing the root surface and contributing to the black triangle formation. Tooth tooth #35, the lower left second premolar, is absent, leaving a gap in the mandibular arch that is filled by soft tissue and the adjacent teeth. An incisal edge defect is visible on one of the anterior teeth, appearing as a chippedous chip or wear facet on the biting edge, which disrupts the smooth contour of the incisal line. ground_truth:
{"description": "In the frontal intra‑oral photograph the viewer sees the entire anterior segment of both arches, with a dental shade guide placed prominently in the middle of the frame serving as a reference for colour matching. The maxillary arch is displayed from right canine across to the left canine region, while the mandibular anterior teeth are visible from the right lateral incisor to the left lateral incisor. The gingival tissues surrounding the present teeth appear pink, firm and scalloped, with healthy‑looking interdental papillae that fill the embrasures. The labial mucosa and the vermilion border of the lips are not obscured, giving a clear view of the dentition.

On the right side of the maxilla the canine (#13) shows a noticeable yellowish hue compared with the surrounding teeth, suggesting intrinsic discoloration. The remaining maxillary incisors on that side look clinically normal in shape, size and colour, with smooth enamel surfaces and no evident restorations. Moving to the left side of the maxillary arch, the central incisor (#21) and lateral incisor (#22) are absent; in their place implant abutments are visible, appearing as metallic, screw‑type fixtures emerging from the gingival tissue and supporting provisional crowns that blend with the adjacent teeth. Further back in the left maxilla, the anterior teeth are missing altogether, leaving a gap that reveals the underlying alveolar ridge, which appears well healedated and free of ulceration.

The mandibular anterior teeth on both sides appear intact, with the incisors displaying a uniform shade that matches the middle tones of the shade guide. No obvious carious lesions or fractures restorations are seen on these lower teeth. However, a thin, white plaque layer is evident at the cervical margins of most of the anterior teeth, forming a faint rim around each tooth near the gum line. This plaque accumulation is uniform and suggests a need for improved oral hygiene, but there are no signs of gingival inflammation such as swelling or bleeding.

Overall the photograph captures a fairly typical anterior dentition apart from the noted yellow discoloration of tooth #13, the implant abutments replacing teeth #21 and #22, the absence of the left maxillary anterior teeth, and the cervical plaque. The shade guide in the centre provides a useful reference for assessing the colour of the existing teeth and planning any future aesthetic work."}

ground_truth:
{"description": "In the frontal occlusal photograph the maxillary and mandibular arches are displayed side‑by‑side, with the occlusal surfaces of all present teeth clearly visible. The most striking feature is the absence of the four maxillary anterior teeth: the right central incisor (#11), right lateral incisor (#12), left central incisor (#21) and left lateral incisor (#22) are missing, leaving a broad, symmetrical edentulous space across the front of the upper arch. The surrounding maxillary canines (#13 on the right and #23 on the left) are present, but each shows a distinct root exposure at the cervical area, especially noticeable on the right canine where the gingival margin is receded to expose the root surface. The right canine (#13) also displays a small chip on its incisal edge and a thin line of white plaque with a modest deposit of calculus at the gingival margin, giving the tooth a slightly rough, matte appearance at the gum line. The left canine (#23) shows a similar root exposure but no obvious plaque or chipping. 

Moving to the posterior maxillary teeth, the premolars and molars appear well aligned, with normal crown morphology, intact occlusal anatomy, and healthy gingival margins that are pink, stippled and free of inflammation. No additional calculus, caries or restorations are evident in the upper arch beyond what has already been described. 

In the mandibular arch the central incisors (#31 and #41) are present but their crowns are noticeably elongated relative to the roots, giving them an unfavorable crown‑to‑root ratio; the roots appear short and the crowns extend further apically than usual, suggesting a compromised periodontal support. The mandibular right lateral incisor (#42) shows localized gingival inflammation and slight bleeding on gentle, with the surrounding gingiva appearing erythematous and ed. A similar inflammatory response is observed around the left mandibular canine (#33), where the gingival tissue is also reddened and bleeds readily, indicating localized periodontal irritation. 

The mandibular right first premolar (#44) exhibits a clear band of white plaque and a thin calculus of deposit at its gingival margin, and like the right canine, its cervical root surface is exposed due to gingival recession. The remaining mandibular premolars and molars display normal occlusal anatomy, appropriate interc‑to‑cusp relationships, and healthy pink gingiva with no signs of plaque. 

Overall, the photograph reveals a well‑organizeded set of posterior teeth with normal occlusion, while the anterior maxillary segment is characterized by the four missing incisors, and the anterior mandibular segment shows compromised crown‑to‑root ratios and localized ging inflammation. The soft tissues, apart from the noted inflamed sites, appear healthy and the oral cavity is otherwise free of overt abnormalities abnormalities."}

ground_truth:
{"description": "The image presents a clear lingual view of the entire mandibular dental arch, captured with the patient’s mouth open to expose the lingual surfaces of all permanent and primary teeth, as well as the surrounding soft tissues. The mandibular alveolar ridge appears smooth and well contoured, with a healthy pink gingival margin that follows the cervical outlines of the teeth. The floor of the mouth is visible in the lower central region, showing a thin, moist mucosa and a modestly positioned lingual frenum that does not impinge on the teeth. The tongue is retracted to the side, allowing an unobstructed view of the lingual pits, fissures, and interproximal areas.

Among the permanent dentition, the mandibular first molars on both sides are discernible. Their occlusal surfaces display darkened stains within the deep lingual pits and fissures, indicating the presence of carious lesions. The right mandibular second primary molar also shows similar blackening in its lingual pits, consistent with early decay. In addition, the primary tooth designated as #3E exhibits a distinct proximal carious cavity; the lesion is evident as a localized loss of enamel and dentin at the side of the tooth, with the cavity margins clearly visible against the surrounding healthy enamel.

The primary second molars on both sides (#33 and #43) are seen erupt transgingival, not yet fully erupted into the oral cavity. Tooth #33 is notably rotated, its crown oriented buccally rather than following the normal mesial‑distal alignment, giving it a consp appearance in the lingual view. Tooth #43, while also transgingival, appears more upright but still partially covered by the alve gingival tissue.

Both mandibular second primary molars exhibit pronounced attrition on their occlusal surfaces, with flattened cusps and worn enamel that suggest long‑standing functional wear. The wear facets are smooth and rounded, indicating a gradual attritional process rather than abrupt loss.

Overall, the photograph captures a generally healthy mandibular arch with normal soft‑tissue architecture, while highlighting specific pathological findings: proximal caries on #3E, lingual pit caries on the bilateral first molars and the right second primary molar, rotated and partially erupted #33, and marked attrition on the second primary molars."}

ground_truth:
{"description": "In this frontal intra‑oral photograph the patient’s dentition is captured during active orthodontic treatment with a conventional metal appliance. Every labial and buccal surface of the teeth is covered with metal brackets, each slot filled with a thin archwire that runs across the arches arch, giving the smile a uniformly metallic sheen appearance. The brackets are securely bonded to the enamel, and the archwire appears to be engaged in the anterior segment, indicating that the treatment is in its alignment phase.

A noticeable diastema separates the left‑side maxillary anterior teeth, creating a small gap between the central incisor and its neighboring tooth. This spacing is clearly visible because the metal brackets outline each tooth’s contour, and the space is accentuated by the surrounding soft tissue.

The gingival margins of the maxillary anterior teeth are infl erythematous and appear swollen, consistent with gingivitis. The gingiva in this region looks inflamed, with a bright hue and slight edema, especially around the brackets where plaque accumulation is likely. In contrast, the attached gingiva of the posterior teeth and the mandibular arch looks generally healthy in color and thickness, although a diffuse black pigmentationation is evident across much of the attached gingiva, reflecting pigment deposition that is common in individuals with melanin pigmentation or due to smoking or certain medications.

Adjacent to many of the metal brackets, especially in the premolar and molar zones, there are faint white spot lesions on the enamel surface. These opacities suggest early demineralization or incipient caries, likely related to plaque retention around the brackets. In addition, subtle brownish pigment streaks are visible on the same enamel surfaces, indicating extrinsic staining from dietary chromogens that have adhered to the bracket‑to‑tooth interface.

Aside the remaining oral structures appear within normal limits: the posterior teeth exhibit proper occlusal contacts, the occlusal surfaces show no gross carious lesions, and the buccal mucosa, lips, and vermolar ridge appear pink and intact. The tongue is not prominently visible in this frontal view, but the floor of the mouth seems unremarkable. Overall, the image captures a patient in mid‑orthodontic therapy with typical appliance‑related findings—metal brackets, a left‑side diastema, inflamed gingival margins, early enamel changes, and gingival pigmentation pigment—while the rest of the oral cavity appears generally healthy."}

ground_truth:
{"description": "The image presents a true‑lateral (90‑degree) intraoral photograph of the left side of the maxillary and mandibular arches captured in centric occlusion. The upper and lower dentitions are in full contact, allowing a clear view of the occlusal relationship and the interarch spacing. The crownsic alignment of the teeth appears generally normal, with each crown exhibitingally oriented and no obvious malposition or crowding evident in the photographed region. The enamel surfaces of the incisors, canines, premolars and molars look intact and glossy, suggesting no overt carious lesions or extensive restorations.

A striking feature throughout both arches is generalized gingival recession. The gingival margins are positioned apical to the cementoen–enamel junction on most teeth, exposing varying lengths of the root surfaces. The recession is most pronounced on the anterior teeth, where the soft tissue appears thin and tapered, revealing smooth, dull‑gray dentin on the exposed roots. No signs of active inflammation such as swelling, eryation or bleeding are apparent; the recessed tissue color is pale pink, consistent with atrophic but non‑inflamed gingiva.

Specifically, in the maxillary left quadrant the canine (#23) and first premolar (#24) exhibit marked papillary atrophy. The interdental papillae between these teeth are flattened reduced, creating conspicuous black triangles that extend from the contact points toward the gingival margin. The loss of papillae gives the interproximal areas a cle, shadowed appearance, emphasizing the recession and the underlying root exposure.

In the mandibular left quadrant, the first premolar (#34) and second premolar (#35) show visible interproximal deposits. These appear as whitish, gritty accumulations along the contact surfaces, consistent with plaque biofilm and early calculus formation. The deposits are localized to the mesial and distal aspects of the two premolars, while the surrounding gingiva remains recessed but otherwise unremarkable.

Aside these findings, the rest of the oral cavity appears within normal limits. The buccal and lingual mucosa are smooth and pink, the floor of the mouth is clear, and the tongue is not visible in this lateral view. No dental appliances, prosthetic crowns, or orthodontic brackets are evident in the captured area. Overall, the photograph highlights generalized gingival recession with focal papillary loss and interproximal plaque/calculus on the left side, set against a backdrop of otherwise normal dentition and oral soft tissues."}

The occlusal surfaces of the central incisors and lateral incisors are smooth and exhibit the expected anatomy of a functional bite, with no obvious overjet, underjet, or malocclusion. The gingival margins are scalloped, and the interdental papillae fill the embrasures adequately, suggesting a normal periodontal status. No dental instruments, retractors, or adjunctive devices are visible in the frame, and the overall oral environment appears clean and well‑maintained."}

On the left side of the arch, the first premolar (#24) displays a black carious lesion confined to its occlusal pits and fissures, mirroring the decay seen on #16. Overall, the image presents a mix of normal anatomical anatomy and specific pathological including three occlusal carious lesions, cervical plaque/calculus on two teeth, a missing tooth (#13), a large interdental space at #12, and an incompletely erupted #23."}

ground_truth: {"description": "The photograph presents a direct frontal view of a patient’s dentition, with the lips gently retracted to expose both the maxillary and mandibular arches. The image is split into two halves: the left side captures the pre‑treatment condition, while the right side shows the same mouth during active orthodontic therapy. On the untreated side, the incis incisors appear slightly rotated and mildly crowded, especially in the anterior segment, and the arch seems a bit narrow, though the overall occlusion of the premolars and molars remains within a Class I relationship. The gingival tissues are pink, firm, and free of inflammation, and no restorations, carious lesions, or periodontal defects are evident. \n\nOn the treatment side, every tooth is fitted with metal brackets that are bonded to the labial surfaces. A thin, silver‑colored stainless‑steel archwire is threaded through the bracket slots, forming a smooth, continuous curve across the dental arch, indicating that the appliance is fully engaged. The brackets are uniformly placed, reflecting the same bracket design throughout the dentition. Beneath the two halves, three small icons illustrate different bracket types: a conventional 0.022‑inch slot metal bracket, a low‑profile ceramic‑metal hybrid bracket, and a self‑ligating metal bracket, suggesting the range of options available to the clinician. The soft tissues on the treated side appear unchanged from the untreated side, with relaxed lips and a tongue resting naturally behind the lower incisors. Overall, the image vividly demonstrates the transition from a crowded, untreated dentition to a mouth that is now under active orthodontic control with metal brackets and an archwire in place."}

{"description": "The photograph captures a close‑up view of the patient’s smile taken from the left side, with a deep black background that isolates the oral structures. The maxillary anterior segment dominates the frame, showing the upper incisors and canines in clear detail, while the lower anterior teeth are only partially visible at the lower edge of the image. The upper lip is slightly retracted, exposing the labial surfaces of the teeth, and the gingival tissues appear healthy, pink, and scalloped with no signs of inflammation or recession in the areas that are visible.

Tooth #12, the maxillary right lateral incisor, draws immediate attention because its labial surface displays a distinct linear crack that runs obliquely across the enamel. The crack is highlighted by a surrounding halo of yellowish discoloration, suggesting localized staining or possible early carious involvement, although the surrounding enamel looks otherwise intact. No adjacent soft‑tissue ulcerular changes or tenderness abnormalities are evident around this lesion tooth.

Moving laterally, the space between tooth #23 (the maxillary left canine) and tooth #24 (the maxillary left first premolar) is noticeably wide, indicating a pronounced interproximal diastema. The diastema appears symmetrical and the surrounding gingiva is normal, with no signs of periodontal pocketing or bone loss.

The remaining maxillary anterior teeth—central incisors and the left canine—appear well aligned, with smooth, glossy enamel surfaces and no visible caries, fractures restor, or attrition. The incisal edges are crisp, and the occlusal relationships seem normal for a natural smile. The mandib mandibular anterior teeth that peek into view are modest in size, with healthy labial gingiva and no evident crowding or missing teeth.

No dental instruments, retract mirrors, or retractors are present in the frame, suggesting that this image was taken for diagnostic documentation rather than during an active procedure. Overall, the photograph provides a clear view of the patient’s dentition, highlighting a cracked and discolored #12 and a pronounced spacing between #23 and #24, while the rest of the oral structures appear within normal limits."}

{"description": "In the lateral intraoral photograph the view captures the maxillary dentition from the right first molar (#16) across the arch to the left second molar (#27) in a labial‑buccal orientation, with a portion of the tongue visible on the lower left side. The second molar on the right side (#17) is absent, leaving a smooth edentulous ridge where the tooth would normally be situated; the surrounding alveolar bone and gingiva appear well‑contoured without signs of pathology.

Moving toward the midline, the central incisor (#21) and the adjacent lateral incisor (#22) display distinct white, chalky patches on their labial surfaces. These areas look like localized enamel hypomineralization, presenting as irregular, opaque zones that contrast with the surrounding healthy enamel. No other discolorations, cracks, or restorations are noted on the remaining teeth in the field, including the premolars (#24, #25) and molars (#26, #27), which all show normal anatomy, proper occlusal anatomy, and smooth, glossy enamel surfaces.

{"description": "The intraoral photograph is taken from a frontal perspective with the maxilla and mandible slightly opened, allowing a clear view of the entire dentition from the anterior incisors to the posterior molars in both arches arches. Both #13 and #23 also show gingival recession that has exposed a portion of their roots, and fine hairline cracks are visible on their labial enamel. The mandibular arch mirrors the maxillary irregularities. The anterior mandibular teeth are misaligned: the right mandibular incisors are inclined mesially, while the left mandibors are tipped distally, creating a scissor‑like relationship across the midline. Gingival recession is present on teeth #43 and #46, exposing portions of the root surfaces. Fine cracks are also observed on the labial surfaces of #43 and #32. Tooth #32 shows a generalized darkening on its distal‑labial surface, raising suspicion for a distal‑ carious lesion. Across the entire dentition, there is marked incisal and occlusal wear, suggesting long‑standing attrition or abrasion habits.

In the posterior mandibular regions bilaterally, the photograph suggests the presence of fixed prosthetic restorations, likely crowns or bridges, although the confidence in this observation is lower. These restorations appear to blend with the surrounding dentition but may be slightly more opaque than the natural teeth.

The key is placed in a way that suggests the clinician device is either being activated or has been recently adjusted, as the screw shows a slight opening of the palatal halves.

A pair of dental forceps can be glimps faintly observed near the lower right corner of the image, although the confidence in this identification is low. ground_truth: {"description": "The photograph captures a frontal view of the patient's patient's maxillary anterior dentition extending slightly into the mandibular anterior region. The upper front teeth are generally well aligned, though there is a pronounced midline diastema separating the right central incisor (#11) from its left counterpart (#21). The labial gingiva appears pink and healthy overall, with only mild areas of erythema adjacent to the cervical margins of several teeth. {"description": "The radiograph is a submental lateral view taken from the patient’s left side at roughly a right‑angle to the occlusal plane, allowing a clear profile of the maxillary and mandibular arches. Both arches are fully equipped with orthodontic brackets and archwires, which are evident as radiopaque bands attached to the crowns of the teeth and thin metallic lines running across the dental arches. The brackets are uniformly positioned on the buccal surfaces of the incis teeth, and the wires appear to be engaged in a continuous sequence from the molars to the incisors, suggesting an ongoing comprehensive orthodontic treatment.

The skeletal relationship is strikingly altered: the mandible projects forward relative to the maxilla, producing a classic reverse bite or underbite. The mandibular body is visibly more anterior than the maxillary alveolar ridge, indicating mandibular prognathism accompanied by maxillary retrusion. This forward positioning of the chin is reflected in the profile of the mandible, which appears more and more prominent placed than would be expected in a normal occlusion.

Despite the skeletal discrepancy, the individual teeth seem to be well aligned within each arch, likely a result of the orthodontic appliances. The maxillary teeth are relatively upright and the occlusal surfaces of the upper molars are in contact with the lower molars, but the contact occurs farther back on the lower arch due to the forward positioning of the mandible. The alveolar bone surrounding the roots appears intact and of normal density, with no evident radiolucencies or pathological lesions.

The temporomandibular joint on the left side is visualized in profile; the condylar head sits within the glenoid fossa in a position consistent with a forward‑projected mandible, but the joint space looks uniform and there are no signs of degenerative changes. The cervical spine and the hyoid bone are within normal limits, and the soft‑tissue outline of the neck is unremarkable. The maxillary sinus on the left appears aerated and of typical size, with well‑defined borders. No foreign bodies or additional dental restorations are apparent beyond the orthodontic hardware.

Overall, the image captures a patient undergoing active orthodontic therapy, with a pronounced mandibular prognathism resulting in an underbite, while the surrounding osseous and soft‑tissue structures appear normal."}

The overall alignment of the arch appears symmetrical, with the majority of the teeth exhibiting normal crown morphology, appropriate incopalatal contours, and well‑maintained occlusal surfaces.

A healing abutment is clearly visible on tooth 21, indicating recent implant‑related surgery; the component sits atop the crown of the left maxillary central incisor and shows a smooth, metallic sheen consistent with a provisional prosthetic element. Adjacent to this, the mesial incisal edge of tooth 11 displays a subtle defect in its incisal angle, suggesting a slight wear or fracture that has altered the sharpness of the incisal tip.

The lower anterior segment (teeth 32 to 42) demonstrates noticeable crowding. The incisors are positioned closely together, with minimal interdental spacing and a slight overlap of the labial surfaces, giving the impression of a compressed arch form. Despite this crowding, the gingival margins appear generally healthy, except for localized recession. Gingival recession is observed on the maxillary right lateral incisor (tooth 22) and canine (tooth 23), as well as on the mandibular right lateral incisor (tooth 33), the mandibular right central incisor (tooth 31), and the mandibular left central and lateral incisors (teeth 41, 42, and 43). The recession areas show a mild apical shift of the gingival margin, exposing a thin band of dentin that is slightly darker than the surrounding enamel, but there is no evident plaque accumulation or inflammation.

The remaining gingival tissues around the other anterior teeth appear pink, firm, and scalloped in a normal fashion, with no signs of swelling, ulceration, or pathological pathological excessive probing depths. No orthodontic appliances, brackets, or removable prostheses are seen in the field. The occlusal relationship seems to be a slight overjet with the maxillary incisors positioned marginally ahead of the mandibular incisors, which is typical for a natural anterior bite.

Overall, the image conveys a well‑captured view of the anterior dentition, highlighting a healing abutment on tooth 21, a mesial incisal angle defect on tooth 11, crowding of the lower anterior teeth (32‑42), and localized gingival recession on the specified teeth, while the rest of the oral structures appear within normal limits."}
